content:
# Joshua Chapter 24 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pages 248-249 | **Source Image:** `../../source-texts/hebrew-OT/JOS_024_page_001.jpg`

---

*[The Covenant at Shechem — Thirty-three verses that form the theological climax of the entire book of Joshua. This is the final assembly, the farewell address, and the last great choice. Joshua gathers all the tribes to Shechem — the place where Abraham first received the promise of the land, where Jacob buried foreign gods, where the bones of Joseph will be laid to rest. Here YHWH himself speaks in the first person through Joshua, recounting the entire sweep of Israel's story from Terah beyond the River to the conquest just completed. Then comes the challenge that echoes across all of biblical history: choose this day whom you will serve. Joshua's famous declaration — "As for me and my household, we will serve YHWH" — is not a greeting card sentiment. It is a covenant ultimatum spoken by a dying man who knows exactly what faithfulness costs. The chapter closes with three burials: the covenant under an oak, Joshua in his inheritance, and the bones of Joseph finally home. PNEUMA renders the whole in the voice of a people standing at a crossroads, hearing the full weight of their story told back to them before they must decide.]*

---

**1** Joshua gathered all the tribes of Israel at Shechem. He summoned the elders, the leaders, the judges, and the officials of Israel, and they presented themselves before God. **2** Joshua said to the whole people: "This is what YHWH, the God of Israel, says: 'Long ago your ancestors — Terah the father of Abraham and the father of Nahor — lived beyond the Euphrates, and they served other gods. **3** But I took your father Abraham from beyond the River and led him through the whole land of Canaan. I made his descendants many; I gave him Isaac. **4** To Isaac I gave Jacob and Esau. To Esau I gave the hill country of Seir as his own, but Jacob and his sons went down to Egypt.

**5** "'Then I sent Moses and Aaron, and I struck Egypt with the plagues I inflicted on them, and afterward I brought you out. **6** I brought your fathers out of Egypt, and you came to the sea. The Egyptians pursued your fathers with chariots and horsemen to the Sea of Reeds. **7** When they cried out to YHWH, he put darkness between you and the Egyptians and brought the sea over them so that it covered them. Your own eyes saw what I did to Egypt. Then you lived in the wilderness for a long time.

**8** "'I brought you into the land of the Amorites who lived east of the Jordan. They fought against you, and I handed them over to you. You took possession of their land, and I destroyed them before you. **9** Then Balak son of Zippor, king of Moab, set himself against Israel. He sent for Balaam son of Beor to curse you, **10** but I would not listen to Balaam. Instead, he ended up blessing you again and again, and I rescued you from Balak's power.

**11** "'You crossed the Jordan and came to Jericho. The citizens of Jericho fought against you — as did the Amorites, the Perizzites, the Canaanites, the Hittites, the Girgashites, the Hivites, and the Jebusites — and I handed them all over to you. **12** I sent the hornet ahead of you, and it drove out the two Amorite kings before you. It was not by your sword and not by your bow. **13** I gave you a land you had not labored on and cities you had not built, and you settled in them. You are eating from vineyards and olive groves you did not plant.'

**14** "So now — fear YHWH. Serve him with complete integrity and faithfulness. Get rid of the gods your ancestors served beyond the River and in Egypt, and serve YHWH. **15** But if serving YHWH seems wrong to you, then choose today whom you will serve — whether the gods your ancestors served beyond the River, or the gods of the Amorites in whose land you are living. As for me and my household, we will serve YHWH."

**16** The people answered: "Far be it from us to abandon YHWH and serve other gods! **17** It is YHWH our God who brought us and our fathers up from the land of Egypt, from the house of slavery. He is the one who performed these great signs before our eyes and guarded us along the entire road we traveled and among all the peoples through whose territory we passed. **18** YHWH drove out before us all the peoples, including the Amorites who lived in the land. We too will serve YHWH, because he is our God."

**19** Joshua said to the people: "You will not be able to serve YHWH, because he is a holy God. He is a jealous God. He will not forgive your rebellion and your sins. **20** If you abandon YHWH and serve foreign gods, he will turn against you. He will bring disaster on you and consume you, after all the good he has done for you."

**21** The people said to Joshua: "No! We will serve YHWH." **22** Joshua said to the people: "You are witnesses against yourselves that you have chosen YHWH for yourselves, to serve him." They said: "We are witnesses." **23** "Then get rid of the foreign gods that are among you, and turn your hearts to YHWH, the God of Israel." **24** The people said to Joshua: "YHWH our God we will serve, and his voice we will obey."

**25** So Joshua made a covenant with the people that day. At Shechem he established for them a statute and an ordinance. **26** Joshua wrote these words in the Book of the Instruction of God. Then he took a great stone and set it up there under the oak that was in the sanctuary of YHWH. **27** Joshua said to all the people: "Look — this stone will be a witness against us, because it has heard all the words that YHWH spoke to us. It will be a witness against you, so that you do not deny your God."

**28** Then Joshua sent the people away, each to their own inheritance.

**29** After these things, Joshua son of Nun, the servant of YHWH, died. He was one hundred and ten years old. **30** They buried him within the borders of his inheritance at Timnath-Serah, which is in the hill country of Ephraim, north of Mount Gaash.

**31** Israel served YHWH throughout all the days of Joshua and throughout all the days of the elders who outlived Joshua and who had personally experienced everything YHWH had done for Israel.

**32** The bones of Joseph, which the Sons of Israel had brought up from Egypt, they buried at Shechem, in the piece of land that Jacob had bought from the sons of Hamor, Shechem's father, for a hundred qesitah. It became the inheritance of the descendants of Joseph.

**33** Eleazar son of Aaron died, and they buried him at Gibeah, the town of his son Phinehas, which had been given to him in the hill country of Ephraim.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**V. 1 — "Presented themselves before God" (vayyityatzvu lifnei ha'elohim):** The Hithpael of yatzav means to station oneself, to take one's stand — a formal, deliberate act. The phrase lifnei ha'elohim ("before God") rather than lifnei YHWH is notable. Some scholars see this as indicating the presence of the Ark at Shechem for this assembly; others see the more general term 'elohim as appropriate for a scene that includes a review of pagan worship. PNEUMA renders "before God" as it stands in the Hebrew, without specifying the mechanism of divine presence.

**V. 2 — "Beyond the Euphrates" (be'ever hannahar):** Literally "beyond the River." Throughout this speech "the River" (hannahar) means the Euphrates — the great geographical boundary of the ancient Near East. The phrase appears three times in this chapter (vv. 2, 3, 14-15), each time anchoring Israel's origin story in Mesopotamia. YHWH's first act in the narrative is to reach across the River and pull Abraham out of polytheism. PNEUMA specifies "Euphrates" at first mention for English clarity, then uses "the River" subsequently.

**V. 2 — "They served other gods" (va'ya'avdu elohim acherim):** The speech opens with a startling admission: Israel's ancestors were idol-worshippers. Terah, Abraham's father, served other gods in Ur and Haran. This is not a shameful secret but the theological premise of the entire address: everything Israel has comes from YHWH's initiative, not from any ancestral merit. The people were pagans; YHWH chose them anyway. The verb 'avad ("to serve/worship") becomes the keynote of the chapter, appearing twenty times — the same word used for serving YHWH and for serving other gods, making the choice starkly clear.

**V. 7 — "He put darkness between you and the Egyptians":** The Hebrew vayasem ma'aphel ("and he placed darkness") refers to the pillar of cloud/darkness from Exodus 14:19-20 that stood between the Israelite and Egyptian camps. The word ma'aphel denotes thick, heavy darkness — the same root appears in descriptions of the plague of darkness (Exodus 10:22). YHWH's deliverance at the sea involved both concealment and destruction. PNEUMA renders directly, letting the strangeness of "put darkness between you" stand, as the Hebrew does.

**V. 12 — "The hornet" (hattzir'ah):** This is one of the most debated terms in Joshua. The word tzir'ah means "hornet" or "wasp," and it appears in the context of YHWH driving out nations (cf. Exodus 23:28, Deuteronomy 7:20). Three main interpretations exist: (a) literal hornets sent as a plague; (b) a metaphor for panic or terror; (c) a reference to Egypt, since the hornet was a symbol associated with Lower Egypt. Whatever the referent, the theological point is unmistakable: "not by your sword and not by your bow." Victory belonged to YHWH, not to Israelite military prowess. PNEUMA retains "hornet" as the literal meaning, since the Hebrew does not signal metaphor.

**V. 12 — "The two Amorite kings":** This refers to Sihon and Og, the Amorite kings east of the Jordan whose defeat is recounted in Numbers 21. Their mention here connects the Transjordanian conquests to YHWH's agency through the hornet, reinforcing that even the earliest military victories were not Israel's own achievement.

**V. 14 — "With complete integrity and faithfulness" (betamim uve'emet):** Literally "in completeness and in truth." Tamim means whole, complete, without defect — the same word used for a sacrificial animal without blemish. 'Emet means truth, reliability, faithfulness. Together they describe worship that is both internally whole (no divided loyalty) and externally genuine (no pretense). PNEUMA uses "complete integrity and faithfulness" to capture both dimensions.

**V. 15 — "Choose today whom you will serve":** The Hebrew bacharu lakhem hayyom ("choose for yourselves today") uses the reflexive dative lakhem — choose for yourselves, at your own cost and benefit. The verb bachar ("to choose") carries covenantal weight throughout the Hebrew Bible. Joshua presents three options: the gods of Mesopotamia (ancestral tradition), the gods of Canaan (cultural pressure), or YHWH (covenantal demand). The framing implies that not choosing is itself a choice — for the default gods of the land. PNEUMA renders the challenge in direct, confrontational English, because that is the tone of the Hebrew.

**V. 15 — "As for me and my household":** The Hebrew va'anokhi uveiti places the independent pronoun anokhi first for emphasis — "But I" — creating a dramatic contrast with whatever the people choose. Joshua does not wait for the people's answer. His decision is already made. The word bayit ("house/household") includes family, servants, and all dependents — an entire social unit covenanted to YHWH.

**V. 19 — "You will not be able to serve YHWH":** This is one of the most provocative statements in the Hebrew Bible. After urging the people to choose YHWH, Joshua immediately tells them they cannot do it. The Hebrew lo' tukh'lu la'avod ("you will not be able to serve") is blunt and shocking. Joshua is not being pessimistic; he is being pastoral. He wants the people to understand the weight of what they are committing to. YHWH is 'el qadosh — a holy God — and 'el qanna' — a jealous God. The doubling of 'el (God) with both adjectives is intensive. Serving this God is not casual; betrayal will have consequences. PNEUMA preserves the shock of the statement without softening it.

**V. 19 — "He will not forgive your rebellion and your sins" (lo'-yissa' lefish'akhem ulekhatto'teikhem):** The verb nasa' ("to lift, carry, forgive") with the negative is stark: he will not lift away your transgressions. Pesha' ("rebellion") and chatta't ("sin") form a standard pair covering willful defiance and moral failure. Joshua is describing what happens when covenant commitment collapses — not that YHWH is incapable of forgiveness, but that covenant violation triggers covenant consequences.

**V. 22 — "Witnesses against yourselves" ('edim attem bakhem):** The people become their own prosecution witnesses. The legal language is deliberate: in covenant-making, witnesses are needed (cf. Deuteronomy 30:19 where heaven and earth serve as witnesses). Here, uniquely, the people who make the commitment are also the witnesses to it. They cannot later claim ignorance. PNEUMA preserves the self-incriminating logic.

**V. 25 — "A statute and an ordinance" (khoq umishpat):** These are two categories of Israelite law. Khoq is a fixed decree, an enacted statute; mishpat is a judicial ruling, an ordinance based on precedent or case law. Together they represent the full scope of covenantal obligation. Joshua did not merely extract a verbal promise — he codified it in legal form.

**V. 26 — "The Book of the Instruction of God" (sefer torat elohim):** Torah here means "instruction" or "teaching," not the later technical designation for the Pentateuch. The phrase indicates a written document associated with the covenant — Joshua adding to an existing body of sacred instruction. The stone witness and the written record together create a double memorial: one visible to the eye, one readable for future generations.

**V. 26 — "Under the oak" (takhat ha'allah):** The word 'allah (or 'elon) refers to a large sacred tree, likely a terebinth or oak. Trees at sanctuaries appear throughout Genesis: Abraham at the oaks of Mamre (Genesis 13:18, 18:1), Jacob burying foreign gods under the oak at Shechem (Genesis 35:4). The echo of Jacob's act in Genesis 35 is deliberate — Joshua is re-enacting the same purification at the same place, the cycle completing itself.

**V. 27 — "It has heard all the words":** Joshua attributes hearing to a stone. This is not animism but legal metaphor elevated to poetry. In the ancient Near East, standing stones served as silent witnesses to treaties. Joshua personifies the stone for rhetorical force: the stone has heard, the stone will testify, the stone will outlast human memory and human faithfulness. The verb sham'ah ("heard") is the same verb used for Israel's central confession: "Hear, O Israel." Even the stone hears better than a forgetful people.

**V. 29 — "One hundred and ten years old":** The age of 110 was considered the ideal lifespan in Egyptian tradition — the same age at which Joseph died (Genesis 50:26). The echo is not accidental. Joseph's story began the descent into Egypt; Joshua's story completes the return. Both men die at 110, forming a literary frame around the entire Egypt-wilderness-conquest arc.

**V. 31 — "The elders who outlived Joshua":** The Hebrew uses the verb he'erikhu yamim acharei Yehoshua — literally "who lengthened days after Joshua." This verse is both a tribute and a warning. Israel's faithfulness lasted exactly as long as the eyewitnesses lived. The implied question hanging over the end of the book is: what happens when the last person who saw YHWH's mighty acts dies? The answer is the book of Judges.

**V. 32 — "The bones of Joseph":** Genesis 50:25 records Joseph's dying request: "God will surely attend to you; bring my bones up from here." Exodus 13:19 records Moses fulfilling the first half: taking the bones out of Egypt. Now, finally — after the wilderness wandering, the Jordan crossing, and the conquest — Joseph's bones are laid to rest at Shechem, in the very plot Jacob purchased. The journey that began with Joseph sold into slavery by his brothers near Shechem (Genesis 37:12-28) ends with his remains returning to that same place. The land Jacob bought from Hamor's sons becomes Joseph's eternal resting place. The narrative circle is complete.

**V. 32 — "A hundred qesitah":** The qesitah is an ancient unit of value whose exact weight is unknown. It appears only three times in the Hebrew Bible (Genesis 33:19, Job 42:11, and here). Its archaic quality is the point — this is an old transaction, going back to the patriarchal period.

**V. 33 — "Gibeah of Phinehas":** The book ends not with Joshua's burial but with Eleazar's, linking the priestly succession to the narrative closure. Phinehas son of Eleazar is the priest whose zeal was demonstrated at Baal-Peor (Numbers 25:7-13). The mention of his town in the final verse of Joshua connects the priestly line to the land, ensuring that both political leadership (Joshua's legacy) and priestly leadership (Eleazar-Phinehas) are anchored in the inheritance.

**On the structure of YHWH's speech (vv. 2-13):** The divine recitation moves through six stages of Israel's history: (1) patriarchal origins in Mesopotamia (vv. 2-4), (2) the Exodus (vv. 5-7), (3) the Transjordanian conquest (vv. 8-10), (4) the crossing of the Jordan and Jericho (v. 11), (5) YHWH's supernatural agency through the hornet (v. 12), and (6) the gift of the land (v. 13). At every stage, YHWH is the subject of the active verbs: I took, I gave, I sent, I struck, I brought, I handed over, I drove out. The people are consistently the objects — passive recipients of divine action. This grammatical pattern is the theological argument: you owe everything to me; now choose.

**On the three-fold exchange (vv. 16-24):** The covenant dialogue has three rounds. In the first (vv. 16-18), the people enthusiastically commit. In the second (vv. 19-21), Joshua pushes back, warning them of the consequences, and they reaffirm. In the third (vv. 22-24), Joshua makes them witnesses against themselves, demands the removal of foreign gods, and they commit a final time. Each round intensifies the gravity. This is not easy belief. It is a commitment extracted under the full weight of its own implications.

— PNEUMA
